data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Global Stock Selloff Continues Amid Trade War Fears
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

Germany manufacturing orders; France industrial production index; trading updates from Sodexo, Norwegian Air

Opening Call:

European stock futures were lower Friday, as a global selloff in stocks continued in the wake of Trump's tariff plans. The dollar and Treasury yields fell; while oil and gold futures also declined.

Equities:

Stock futures fell early Friday, pointing to a continued selloff in European markets at the open as investors continued to seek shelter in haven assets following President Trump's announcement of sweeping U.S. tariffs.

Some of America's allies came out swinging after details of the U.S. president's tariff plans were disclosed. French President Emmanuel Macron said Europe is weighing retaliation against U.S. tech firms, while Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said his country will match U.S. auto tariffs with 25% tariffs of its own.

Attention is also on U.S. jobs data and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech later today.

"Markets will be focused on any comments that Powell makes in regards to the impacts of tariffs on the U.S. economy and inflation," said Commonwealth Bank of Australia's Kristina Clifton.

Forex:

The dollar's safe-haven status was missing in a day dominated by a flight for safety in global markets.

"There is a risk that major shifts in capital flow allocations take over from currency fundamentals and that FX moves become disorderly," Deutsche Bank's George Saravelos said. "Expect pushback. We are in the midst of dramatic regime change in markets," he said.

Bonds:

A flight to safety and quality resulted in rising demand for government debt in the U.S., U.K., Germany, Japan and Australia - which sent yields down across all those countries.

"Part of the reason why there's a flight to quality is because of growth concerns. We're seeing that in almost every government bond market in the world," said Tom Nakamura, currency strategist and co-head of fixed income at AGF Investments.

But a sudden reversal of Trump's tariff plans based on further negotiations with other countries down the road may risk turning the rally into a selloff at some point, which would burn the investors and traders who are now rushing into the U.S. bond market.

Energy:

Oil fell early Friday, weighed by U.S. tariffs and OPEC+'s supply increase. The U.S. tariffs and retaliation from targeted countries suggest that crude oil demand will be weaker than previously forecast, TD Securities' Bart Melek said.

Demand will probably increase by less than 1 million barrels per day, while supply will likely grow by "materially" more than that, resulting in a robust surplus over next nine months, he said. There may be "more (oil price) downside in the cards," Melek added.

Metals:

"[Gold] could remain on a bullish trend as investors could continue to seek safety in the wake of President Trump's tariff announcement," Exness' Inki Cho said.

-

Copper prices fell in Asian trading. Investors fear that the new tariffs imposed by Trump will weaken demand for industrial commodities, ANZ said. This could be worsened if affected countries retaliate, leading to a global trade war.

While metals were exempt from these latest tariffs, they face separate measures to help boost U.S. supply, ANZ said.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

The Rest of the World Is Bracing for a Flood of Cheap Chinese Goods

President Trump's jumbo tariffs on China threaten to create a new problem for a global economy already stressed over trade: a $400 billion deluge of Chinese goods looking for new markets.

U.S. consumers and businesses learned Wednesday that, from April 9, Chinese imports will face tariffs of around 70% on average, after Trump walloped China with stiff new duties as part of his "Liberation Day" trade broadside. The new tariffs will likely push up prices in the U.S. for products ranging from consumer electronics and toys to machinery and essential components for manufacturing.

The Day Trump's Tariffs Shook Corporate America

Business leaders scrambled Thursday to assess how President Trump's latest tariffs would affect their companies, with many top CEOs staying silent as the president's new policies made the cost of doing business in the U.S. much steeper.

Restoration Hardware Chief Executive Gary Friedman was on a call with analysts Wednesday afternoon talking about the furniture retailer's strategy and how the housing market could slow in this economic environment when news of the tariffs broke. He asked colleagues to get a "live read" of his business to check how the company's stock was doing.

Resistance Is Futile, Make a Deal: Trump's Tariff Message to the World

Leaders from Canada, Europe and China are threatening stiff countermeasures against the U.S. in response to President Trump's surprisingly steep tariffs on nearly all imports. The administration's response is, don't even think about it.

Trump is trying to short-circuit the trade war's cycle of retaliation by threatening massive new tariffs on any country that responds, and by dangling the prospect of a better deal for those who hold their fire and negotiate. The highest tariffs-the so-called reciprocal duties for many countries with goods-trade imbalances with the U.S.-don't go into effect until Wednesday, giving world leaders time to plead their cases with a president who considers himself a master dealmaker.

AppLovin Confirms Indication of Interest in TikTok Purchase

AppLovin confirmed that it has expressed interest in acquiring TikTok in all markets outside of China to President Trump as the deadline to sell or shut the social-media app approaches.

The mobile advertising technology company on Thursday said that the indication of interest it provided is preliminary.

Bank Stocks Get Walloped. It's An Alarm Bell For Investors.

Bank and brokerage stocks fell sharply Thursday as investors weighed a gloomy outlook and the economic ramifications of President Donald Trump's far-reaching tariffs.

The KBW Nasdaq Bank Index ended the day down 9.9%. Shares of the nation's largest consumer banks were among those hardest hit. Bank of America's stock plunged 11%. Shares of Wells Fargo and JPMorgan Chase were down 9% and 7%, respectively.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Expected Major Events for Friday

05:45/SWI: Mar Unemployment

06:00/GER: Feb Manufacturing orders

06:00/GER: Feb Manufacturing turnover

06:30/HUN: Feb Preliminary Industrial Production

06:45/FRA: Feb Industrial production index

07:00/CZE: Feb Retail trade

07:00/SPN: Feb Industrial Production

08:00/UK: Mar UK monthly car registrations figures

08:00/ITA: Feb Retail Sales

08:30/UK: Mar S&P Global UK Construction PMI

09:00/ITA: 4Q General Government Quarterly Accounts

09:00/EU: 4Q Quarterly sectoral accounts

09:00/EU: 4Q Quarterly Balance of Payments

10:00/IRL: Mar Irish Live Register latest monthly figures

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: EPE Special Opportunities Plans Share Buyback
data.description:
EPE Special Opportunities plans to repurchase up to 2% of its issued share capital.

The private equity investor said Thursday the buyback will be funded through its cash reserves and the repurchased shares will be held in treasury.

data.title: Eni Plans New EUR1.5 Billion Share Buyback Program
data.description:
Eni (ENI.MI) plans to launch a new share buyback plan totaling 1.5 billion euros in 2025.

The Italian energy company added in a Thursday release that the program may be increased to 3.5 billion euros in case of upside scenarios for its cash flow from operations.

The shares repurchased under the new program will be cancelled.

data.title: Turkey - Factors To Watch on April 4
data.description:
Here are news, reports and events that may affect Turkish financial markets on Friday.

The lira traded at 38.0 against the U.S. dollar at 0419 GMT, little changed from its close of 38.0105 on Thursday.

The main BIST 100 share index closed 0.41% lower at 9,484.26 points on Thursday.

GLOBAL MARKETS

Stocks limped to the end of the week on Friday, the dollar was set for its worst week in a month while gold flirted with a record peak as investors feared U.S. President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs would tip the global economy into a recession.

FOREIGN MINISTER

Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan will attend the second day of the NATO Foreign Ministers meeting in Brussels.

TRADE MINISTER

Trade Minister Omer Bolat will hold a press conference to announce March trade data. (0730 GMT)

ENERGY MINISTER

Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar will attend meeting of the Southern Gas Cooridor Advisory Board in Baku, as well as a ministerial meeting of the Green Energy Advisory Board. (0700 GMT)

OPPOSITION

Deniz Yucel, spokesperson for the main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP), will speak to reporters after visiting the jailed Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu and others in prison. (0700 GMT)

REAL EFFECTIVE EXCHANGE RATE

The Central Bank will announce the real effective exchange rate for March. (1130 GMT)

PRICE DEVELOPMENTS

The central bank will publish its monthly price development report. (1500 GMT)

INFLATION

Turkish annual consumer price inflation slowed to 38.1% in March, official data showed on Thursday, lower than a Reuters poll forecast and extending its fall from a peak of around 75% last May.

ISRAEL-SYRIA

Israel stepped up airstrikes on Syria, declaring the attacks a warning to the new Islamist rulers in Damascus as it accused their ally Turkey on Thursday of trying to turn the country into a Turkish protectorate.

Turkey said that Israel must withdraw from Syria and stop harming stabilization efforts there, after Israel stepped up air strikes in the neighbouring country and accused Turkey of trying to turn Syria into a Turkish protectorate.

JAILED ISTANBUL MAYOR

Turkish police have detained 11 people as part of an investigation into social media calls for an economic boycott backed by the opposition following the arrest of Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, state media reported on Thursday.

Turkish hot stocks (TR-HOT)

data.title: UAE Non-Oil Private Sector PMI Hits 6-Month Low
data.description: The S&P Global United Arab Emirates PMI edged lower to 54.0 in March 2025 from 55.0 in the previous two months, pointing to the lowest reading since last September.

data.title: Goldman cuts oil price forecasts amid tariff fears, higher OPEC+ supply
data.description:
Goldman Sachs lowered its forecast for Brent crude's average price this year by 5.5% to $69 a barrel and for WTI prices by 4.3% to $66, citing the risks of higher OPEC+ supply and the global trade war triggering a recession.

The Wall Street brokerage also chopped its 2026 average price forecast for Brent by 9% to $62 and for WTI by 6.3% to $59, and warned that the new estimates could be lowered further.

"The risks to our reduced oil price forecast are to the downside, especially for 2026, given growing risks of recession and to a lesser extent of higher OPEC+ supply," Goldman analysts said in a note.

Brent crude was priced at $69.59 a barrel as of 0408 GMT on Friday, while WTI was at $66.39.

Crude prices posted their biggest percentage drops since 2022 on Thursday after U.S. President Donald Trump slapped reciprocal tariffs on many countries and eight OPEC+ members unexpectedly advanced their plan to phase out production cuts by boosting output in May.

The latter, said Goldman, showed OPEC's flexibility to rapidly implement large output hikes, which diminished the likelihood of a price boost in the short term from lower supply.

The brokerage said it expects oil demand to be between 600,000 and 700,000 barrels per day this year, down from its previous forecast of 900,000 barrels.

data.title: Japan February Household Spending Falls in Real Terms Despite Nominal Rise
data.description:
Japanese household spending declined in real terms in February, as rising prices continued to erode purchasing power despite nominal gains, government data showed on Friday.

Average monthly consumption expenditures for two-or-more-person households rose 3.8% in nominal terms from a year earlier to 290,511 yen but fell 0.5% after adjusting for inflation, according to the Internal Affairs Ministry.

The decline marks the first since November 2024, although it was better than the market's estimate of a 1.7% drop.

In worker households, average monthly income climbed 1.9% on a nominal basis to 571,993 yen but declined 2.3% in real terms.

data.title: Market Chatter: More Singapore Firms Seeking Secondary Listings in Malaysia
data.description:
Singapore companies have started seeking secondary listings on the Malaysian bourse, according to a report by the Straits Times on Thursday.

UMS Integration and Grand Venture Technology have already received approvals for secondary listings, the report added.

The secondary listings come as the 2025 market hopes for a rebound following slow IPO activity in recent years, the report noted.

In 2024, the Malaysian bourse recorded a 20-year high in IPOs, thanks to increased foreign investments, the report added.

data.title: Gjensidige Forsikring Approves Merger with Newly Acquired Buysure
data.description:
Gjensidige Forsikring's board approved a plan to merge its newly purchased home insurance Buysure subsidiary into the company.

The final decision on the proposal by the boards of both companies is expected by May 5, with the merger expected to be concluded by July 1, the insurance company said Thursday.

data.title: MSNBC INTERVIEW WITH GOV. JARED POLIS (D-CO)
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 04, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

GOV. JARED POLIS (D-CO)

MSNBC INTERVIEW WITH GOV. JARED POLIS (D-CO)

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

MSNBC INTERVIEW WITH GOV. JARED POLIS (D-CO)

APRIL 4, 2025

SPEAKERS:

GOV. JARED POLIS (D-CO)

ALICIA MENENDEZ, MSNBC HOST

ALICIA MENENDEZ, MSNBC HOST: Joining me now, Colorado Governor Jared Polis.

Governor, thanks for being with us. Your reaction to these tariffs and to the market sell-off.

GOV. JARED POLIS (D-CO): It's a sad day, really one of the saddest days, when we're inflicting — the president of the United States is inflicting this much harm and damage and pain to people, over $3 trillion of value wiped out in the stock market in one day, over $8 trillion over the last few weeks because of people knowing he might do this.

And that's money from people's pensions and retirement and savings. And it's just a sad day for our country and, frankly, also a sad day for the world.

MENENDEZ: Help me understand. When you talk to business owners in your state about this news, what is it they are telling you about the impact it is going to have on Colorado?

POLIS: So I think people understand right away the tax increase for families. It's about $2,100 per family. That's the Tax Foundation, nonpartisan. That's how much it costs the average family.

But what I think people need to understand and we're already seeing is how this will destroy the manufacturing base of our country. What I hear from companies is, we're not going to increase our capacity in the United States. We're going elsewhere because we get parts and materials from other countries. If those are subject to tariffs, we're going to produce them elsewhere, whether it's Canada, whether it's Mexico, whether it's Europe.

So investment and capital and manufacturing jobs are going to increasingly flee our country. Prices will go up for consumers and it will cause a huge recession for us and, of course, to a lesser extent a recession for the world.

It's a disastrous economic policy, and I just was hoping, like many were, that the president was using this as a negotiating tactic. But apparently he believes in a flawed theory that now unfortunately we're going to have a real-world example of how tariffs destroy your manufacturing base, destroy jobs and raise costs for people.

MENENDEZ: Well, especially because that's the opposite of what they're saying it's going to do.

I want you to take a listen to Lutnick just today talking about what all of this is going to mean for American jobs.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

HOWARD LUTNICK, U.S. COMMERCE SECRETARY: When Donald Trump yesterday said we have about $5 trillion of committed investment to America, $5 trillion, that means people are going to start building factories right now. And that means all that construction work begins now. That starts to employ Americans today.

You're going to see plants being built, factories rebuilding. All shifts are going to be running hot across America now. You're going to see employment leaping starting today.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

MENENDEZ: Where is it? When do I start seeing it?

And how are they accounting for the fact that manufacturers — let's say they wanted to re-up manufacturing in this country, and they wanted those jobs to be in this country, and they were even willing to contend with what you laid out, which is, parts are manufactured in different places, then off in assembly, we're going to manufacture them here.

If you were dealing with a world leader who could wake up tomorrow and change his mind, what incentive is there for you to make a capital investment in manufacturing in the United States?

POLIS: That's exactly right.

I mean, what Secretary Lutnick said sounds wonderful. Tragically, it's delusional. And I think there are many members of the administration that I hope are not subject to this delusion that somehow capital is coming to invest. It's the opposite. Capital's being destroyed. We saw $3 trillion destroyed today alone value.

But it also deters future investment because, nobody knows the rules of the game and — with manufacturing in the United States. If they're going to be selling to a world market, they're going to make it somewhere else. They're going to move manufacturing overseas. And that's what we're already seeing, people threatening and talking about pulling their investments out, going elsewhere.

And that's tragically what's already occurring. So, again, people who have a delusional viewpoint will watch again as the unemployment numbers rise, investment flees, people's savings and pensions go down. And I guess then hopefully they will come to realization, too late.

I hope eventually they back off these tariffs, but the uncertainty will have already set in, destroying investment and destroying capital formation.

MENENDEZ: I think you get it really right when you talk about the tax that this is going to mean for American families and the fact that that is how people are going to feel at first, because I think there are people who say, well, the market's the market and perhaps I don't have exposure to the market.

And it's like, no, we all do. And you're going to see this in the cost of goods. You're going to see this in inflationary measures. And you're also going to see it because our allies are no longer so sure that they are our allies.

I want you to take a listen to what the Canadian prime minister had to say today about Canada's relationship to the United States.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

MARK CARNEY, CANADIAN PRIME MINISTER: Our old relationship of steadily deepening integration with the United States is over. The 80-year period when the United States embraced the mantle of global economic leadership, when it forged alliances rooted in trust and mutual respect, and championed the free and open exchange of goods and services is over.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

MENENDEZ: How do you explain to the America first crowd that America first is becoming America alone?

POLIS: Well, and it's becoming America last, as the rest of the world works together to build prosperity and wealth and jobs, and apparently America not only chooses not to be part of it, to the detriment of our own people, but actively seeks to destroy wealth.

We're a big market. We're not the only market in the world. Goods and manufacturing will go on. European nations will trade with one another. They will trade with Asia, Americans on the sidelines. Families are going to see the immediate increases in cost, $2,100 a year. That's everything from socks to shirts to groceries starting right away.

And too many American families will feel the pain of losing a job and the uncertainty around their employment.

MENENDEZ: There is — as with all things in this administration, there is the doing and then there is the undoing, right? Like, no matter what happens now, there is damage in terms of mutual respect, mutual trust that I don't want to say is irreparable, but has clearly experienced some harm.

Let's say he wakes up in a week and he says, yes, these aren't working, I don't want to do it. To whom will the rebuilding fall and what will that rebuilding require to take, as the Canadian prime minister said, back the mantle of global economic leadership?

POLIS: So, two things.

First of all, again, the uncertainty will set in and stay. The shadow of these actions will last long beyond the tariffs themselves. Let's hope he wakes up tomorrow, next week, realizes these are destroying our economy, reverses them. It'll reverse some of the damage, but not all.

I don't think you would see the stock market, for example, recover the full $3 trillion. You might see it recover half. You might see it recover three-quarters, because people know that they're going to hesitate to invest in a country where they're at the whim of what side of the bed the president wakes up on in the morning, tariffs, not tariffs.

What are we going to do and is our capital investment of millions of dollars in creating jobs going to be justified and stable in a country where we don't even know where we can get our parts and materials in to make the products that we're selling across the United States and across the world?

So, this is going to be devastating for a generation. I hope it leads Congress and the Senate — there was a bipartisan bill dropped today to take back the mantle of Congress being able to prevent tariffs from unilaterally being entered by a president.

It'll eventually take that kind of action to help provide the reliability that we need to attract the investment we need to actually grow our economy, rather than shrink our economy and destroy jobs, which this tariff tax hike will do and already is doing.

MENENDEZ: Colorado Governor Jared Polis, thank you so much for getting us started.

END

data.title: India's Mazagon Dock falls as government plans stake sale at discount
data.description:
** Shares of Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders slide 5.8% to 2,580.1 rupees

** India's government plans to sell up to 4.8% stake in co at 2,525 rupees, a 7.8% discount to last close

** Sale to open for non-retail investors on Friday; retail investors can bid for shares on Monday

** India government owns 85% stake in co

** Stock down 2.5% this week, trims YTD gains to 15.8%

data.title: Vietnam Stocks Point to Steep Losses Weekly
data.description:
Vietnam's shares plunged 36 points, or 3.0%, to 1,193 in early deals on Friday, extending losses from the previous session, following U.S. President Trump's imposition of a hefty 46% tariff on Vietnam.

The VN Index hit its lowest level since August 2024, tracking a slump on Wall Street overnight, amid worries that potential global retaliation could hamper economic growth.

All sectors moved into the red, with the steepest fall for communications.

The U.S. is Vietnam's largest export market, with the trade surplus surpassing USD 123 billion last year.

However, the country maintained its economic growth target of at least 8% this year.

Vietnam's exports to the U.S. account for almost 30% of its GDP. Among the bottom performers were Sunshine Group (-7.8%), TCT Hang Khong (-6.8%), Masan Group Corp. (-5.5%), Petrovietnam Gas (-5.0%), and Saigon Beer (-3.7%).

For the week, the index is heading for a 9.1% plunge amid concerns over a global trade war.

data.title: FX option expiries for 4 April 10am New York cut
data.description:
There are a couple to take note of on the day, as highlighted in bold.

That said, I'll begin with a big caveat in saying that the expiries board don't matter all too much and won't have much, if any, impact on days/weeks like these. It's all market emotion and reaction to key headlines more so than anything else. That overrides the impact on trading sentiment from most of everything else.

So, I wouldn't look too much into the expiries above as we wrap up the trading week.

There are some large ones for EUR/USD at the 1.1050 level and AUD/USD at the 0.6300 level. But with all due respect, they aren't going to factor much into the equation for price action today. On the latter, it sits close to the 100-day moving average at 0.6315 at least. But again, it doesn't mean too much when traders are focusing more on broader market sentiment rather than isolated price instances and individual technical plays for now.

For more information on how to use this data, you may refer to this post here.

data.title: Japan, South Korea to Seek Tariff Relief Amid Growth Risks: ING
data.description:
Japan and South Korea will seek to negotiate lower US reciprocal tariffs of 24% and 25%, respectively, instead of retaliating, ING said in a Thursday note, adding that both are likely to offer concessions such as increased US imports or investments.

While semiconductors and pharma are exempt, machinery and heavy industry will be hit hardest. ING said the tariffs pose downside risks to both economies.

ING still expects a Bank of Japan rate hike in May but sees a risk of delay.

For the Bank of Korea, ING sees a possible rate cut in May, depending on political and foreign exchange market developments.

data.title: Market Chatter: Suntory to Shift to Local Market as Tariff Risks Grow
data.description:
Suntory Beverage & Food will shift to producing and selling spirits locally to counter rising trade tensions, Reuters reported Thursday, citing new president Nobuhiro Torii.

Torii, who took over on March 25, said US tariffs have prompted Suntory to rethink its global supply chain, focusing on local production to avoid excise duties, the report said.

The group has accelerated tequila shipments to the US and may redirect Scotch sales to Europe, Reuters wrote.

While Suntory's soft drinks face less exposure, Torii said the company will prioritize organic growth over acquisitions, according to the report.

data.title: Singapore disappointed with 10% tariffs, will seek negotiation with the US: trade minister
data.description:
Singapore's trade minister said the wealthy financial hub was disappointed that the U.S. had imposed a 10% tariff on its exports despite it having a free-trade agreement and running a trade deficit with the United States.

Singapore could take countermeasures under the free-trade agreement in force since 2004, but has chosen not to do so, Trade Minister Gan Kim Yong told a press conference on Thursday.

"Retaliatory import duties will just add cost to our imports," he said, noting that the government would be reviewing its economic forecasts because of the worsening situation.

Gan said Singapore will try to engage the U.S. to understand President Donald Trump's areas of concern and see if they can be resolved.

"If there are no specific concerns, then it's more difficult to argue or to negotiate," he said.

Singapore was hit by Trump's 10% base tariff on imports, albeit much lower than neighbours in Southeast Asia where six countries were hit with tariffs of between 32% and 49%. The U.S. had a goods trade surplus of $2.8 billion with Singapore last year, an 84.8% increase over 2023, according to the United States Trade Representative website.

Gan, who is also Singapore's deputy prime minister, said the city-state's data showed the U.S. trade surplus with Singapore amounted to a "substantial" $30 billion in 2024. He did not elaborate.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Financial Services News of the Day - Friday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Bank Stocks Get Walloped. It's an Alarm Bell for Investors.

Shares of Bank of America, Morgan Stanley, and other big banks are down as much as 10%.

Blackstone Grants Minnesota Pension an Opt-Out of China in Latest Asia Fund

The Minnesota State Board of Investment approved a commitment of up to $300 million in Blackstone's latest Asia-Pacific fund.

Deloitte to Lay Off U.S. Consultants After Government Cost Crackdown

The move comes after demands by the federal government to identify potential cost cuts to existing projects.

Market Upheaval From Trump's Tariffs Could Be Just the Beginning

Investors who think the return to high tariffs will hammer the economy into recession should expect much bigger falls in stocks and bond yields.

Venture Market Notches Strong Quarter-Thanks Mostly to One Mammoth AI Deal

Investors say competition for artificial intelligence startups is fierce, and OpenAI's $40 billion fundraise proved it.

How the Company Making Nearly All Its Money From the Government Is Navigating Trump's Cuts

Booz Allen's CEO discusses the more than $1 billion in price concessions his firm has offered up and what its business will look like post-DOGE.

Goldman Sachs Is Taking Heat Over Top Executives' Pay

Two influential proxy-advisory groups are advising investors to vote against pay packages for two top executives at the bank's annual shareholder meeting this month.

Consulting Giants Offer Billions in Cuts to Federal Contracts. It Might Not Be Enough.

Accenture, Deloitte, IBM and other firms submitted proposed price concessions but are expected to be asked to dig deeper.

Deutsche Bank's DWS Fined $27 Million in Greenwashing Probe

The asset-management arm of Deutsche Bank agreed to settle an investigation by German prosecutors into allegations that it overstated its environmental, social and corporate-governance practices.

Judges Issue Mixed Rulings on Lawsuits Over Private-Equity Pension Deals

Two federal courts made contradictory decisions on lawsuits claiming Apollo Global Management's insurer Athene is too risky to manage pension assets.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Friday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Oil Futures Slide on U.S. Tariffs, OPEC+ Output Plan

Crude futures suffered their sharpest one-day decline in almost three years as concerns about loss of demand due to U.S. tariffs are exacerbated by OPEC+ speeding up the return of withheld production.

OPEC+ to Make Larger Output Hike in May, Oil Tumbles

Eight OPEC+ countries will increase output by 411,000 barrels a day next month, prompting oil prices to fall further.

Activist Elliott Names Nominees for Phillips 66 Board Amid Proxy Fight

Activist investor Elliott Investment Management disclosed the names of its four nominees to join Phillips 66' board of directors, in a move that comes shortly after it sued the oil refiner and its board for seat changes.

U.S. Natural Gas Stays Focused on Weather, Storage

U.S. natural gas rose as the Trump administration's global tariff plan sent crude and other markets into a tailspin.

Venezuela Bondholders Find Path to Repayment in Citgo Auction

The leading bid for Citgo favors creditors whose collateral rights over the refiner are in dispute.

U.S. Crude Oil Inventories See Large Build

U.S. crude oil inventories increased by 6.2 million barrels last week as imports rose and refineries reduced their capacity use.

Low-Income Heating Program Loses Its Staff in HHS Cutbacks

A spokesman for the Department of Health and Human Services said it will continue to comply with statutory requirements.

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on oil futures, OPEC+ and more in the latest Market Talks covering energy and utilities.

A Big Coal Plant Was Just Imploded to Make Way for an AI Data Center

The country's largest natural-gas power plant is planned east of Pittsburgh.

Tariffs Are Bound to Hit the Oil Patch. It Could Lift Prices, or Tank Them.

Energy products are the U.S.'s biggest exports and among its biggest imports, putting them in the front lines of a potential trade war.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0241 GMT - Palm-oil prices fall in Asia trade, amid overnight weakness in soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade and softer crude-oil prices, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. Lower crude oil prices are weighing on CPO prices, as they reduce palm oil's appeal as a biofuel alternative. AmInvestment Bank sees support for CPO prices at 4,362 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,532 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for June delivery is 114 ringgit lower at 4,375 ringgit a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0202 GMT - Gold is likely to be perceived as a reliable hedge against market volatility, especially amid rising uncertainty under the Trump 2.0 administration, says James Ooi, a market strategist at Tiger Brokers, in an email. "The current gold bull run could be prolonged if trade tensions escalate, including longer-than-expected trade renegotiations and a continued cycle of U.S. and retaliatory tariffs," Ooi says. Also, "short-duration bonds, such as money market funds, may see increased inflows, as they tend to have low correlation with equity markets," Ooi adds. Spot gold is down 0.2% at $3,109.34/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0141 GMT - Copper prices are lower in early Asian trade. Investors fear that the new tariffs imposed by Trump will weaken demand for industrial commodities, ANZ Research analysts write in a note. This could be worsened if impacted countries retaliate, leading to a global trade war. China has already vowed countermeasures on Thursday. While metals were exempt from these latest tariffs, they face separate measures to help boost U.S. supply, they say. Aluminium already has a blanket 25% fee on all U.S. imports, while a tariff on copper is expected within weeks, they add. The three-month LME copper contract is 0.6% lower at $9,310.00 a ton.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0012 GMT - Amid the chaos of President Trump's tariff blitz, the DBS Chief Investment Office says investors should favor equity markets with room for fiscal stimulus and favor companies with capacity to shift production back to the U.S. For credit, investors should favor high quality credit in the A-/BBB-rated space, maintain "duration barbell" of 2- to 3-year and 7- to 10-year, and favor long-duration Treasury Inflation Protected Securities, DBS CIO says. Also, investors should be overweight safe-haven gold as volatility and uncertainty are tailwinds for bullion owing to its "non-market correlating characteristics," DBS CIO adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2343 GMT - Gold is steady in the early Asian session, supported by potential safe-haven demand. "The asset could remain on a bullish trend as investors could continue to seek safety in the wake of President Trump's tariff announcement," Exness' Inki Cho says in an email. The tariffs have damped investor sentiment and spurred demand for safe-haven assets such as gold, the financial markets strategist adds. Spot gold is little changed at $3,114.02/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2340 GMT - Australia's Ansell could face intensifying competition while it implements changes to its sourcing and pricing agreements, Jefferies analysts say. They tell clients in a note that it will take time for the personal-protective equipment manufacturer to implement its response to U.S. tariffs. Writing prior to Ansell's announcement that it will hike its U.S. prices to offset tariff impacts, the Jefferies analysts observe that the company has greatest pricing power in categories where it has leading market share. They also reckon that Ansell has flexibility with outsourced products to pull supply from lower-tariffed countries. Jefferies has a last-published hold rating and A$42.00 target price on the stock, which is up 3.75% at A$30.44. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1938 GMT - Livestock futures on the CME finish lower — with live cattle futures closing down 1.3% to $2.05325 a pound while lean hog futures end down 1.1% to 96.75 cents a pound. A 'bloody' futures market overnight led into selling today, says StoneX in a note. The firm says that one of the challenges faced by the livestock market in today's trading session is divorcing itself from the negative momentum seen in other market segments following Trump's tariff announcement yesterday — which ultimately the livestock complex found itself unable to overcome. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1921 GMT - Crude futures suffer their sharpest one-day decline in almost three years as concerns about loss of demand due to U.S. tariffs are exacerbated by OPEC+ speeding up the return of withheld production. "These withering trade tariff developments arrive at a time when the macroeconomic momentum was already softening," analysts at Citi Research say in a note. "Yet, sanctions on Iranian, Venezuelan, and eventually Russian oil purchases act to tighten supply, thereby offsetting impacts from goods tariffs." Citi keeps its 2Q Brent price forecast at $68 a barrel. Brent settles down 6.4% at $70.14 a barrel, and WTI falls 6.6% to $66.95 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1915 GMT - U.S. natural gas rises as the Trump administration's global tariff plan sends crude and other markets into a tailspin. "The weather is usually the driver of gas pricing and this week's cooldown that appears poised to extend into next week is currently offering support," Ritterbusch says in a note. The EIA reported a 29 Bcf storage build for last week, a third consecutive increase which was within the range of expectations. The early start to the injection season, which typically begins in April, has reduced the deficit over the year-ago and five-year average levels. "We expect a deficit to remain intact throughout this month and any consensus updates suggestive of a hot summer could have an exaggerated price impact," Ritterbusch adds. Natural gas settles up 2% at $4.138/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1909 GMT - A spate of state-level bans or labeling requirements tied to food additives isn't a "material concern" for Conagra, CEO Sean Connolly says on a call with analysts. More than 90% of the Slim Jim and frozen-dinner maker's product portfolio doesn't have synthetic dyes or food colorings, the CEO says. The company will have to monitor the situation, because state-by-state legislation can create challenges for manufacturers, he says. "But with respect to food dyes and colors in general, we don't have a lot of it in our portfolio," Connolly says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1845 GMT - Gold futures tumble, with metals across the board sinking on Trump tariff plans. Gold falls 1.4% to $3,097 a troy ounce, which is still the fourth-highest close for the contract in its history. Industrial metals also slid, which weighed on gold. "Domestic U.S. metals producers are unlikely to be able to ramp up production quickly," says Capital Economics in a note. Silver falls 7.7% to $31.844 per ounce, and copper drops 4.2% to $4.811 a pound. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1801 GMT - Grains are a ripe target for counter-tariff retaliation, leaving agricultural futures particularly vulnerable "I'm afraid some countries will show more aggressive measures against the U.S. that could spur another round of selling over the coming weeks," says Brady Huck of Advance Trading. "That's my fear for American farmers." Most-active corn is down 0.1%, soybeans fall 1.9%, and wheat is down 0.1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: UAE non-oil business sector growth slows in March, PMI shows
data.description:
Growth in the UAE's non-oil private sector moderated in March, a survey showed on Friday, highlighting a softening in demand momentum in the Gulf region's most diversified economy.

The seasonally adjusted S&P Global Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) slipped to 54.0 last month from 55.0 in February, marking the slowest pace of growth since September.

New order growth slowed for the third consecutive month, with the new orders index falling to 56.3 in March from 57.3 the previous month, the weakest reading since October.

"Some firms could be encountering challenges in meeting their sales targets," said David Owen, senior economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Despite the slowdown, businesses ramped up input purchases at the fastest rate since July 2019, aiming to clear backlogs of work. However, employment growth hit its weakest level in nearly three years as firms struggled to find suitable candidates.

Input prices saw a moderate rise, with some firms noting higher material costs while others benefited from lower transport prices.

Dubai's non-oil private sector also experienced a slowdown in March, with the headline PMI falling to a five-month low of 53.2 from 54.3 in February. New orders rose sharply but at a slower rate, prompting a rare reduction in employment.

But UAE firms remain optimistic about future growth, supported by strong pipelines and national infrastructure development.

data.title: Takeovers Panel Probes Jim Grenon's NZME Share Acquisition, Shares Down 3%
data.description:
NZME said the New Zealand Government's Takeovers Panel considers there is a reasonable possibility that certain share acquisitions by James Grenon in the company may not have complied with the Takeovers Code, according to a Friday New Zealand and Australian bourse filing.

The Panel made preliminary inquiries as to whether Grenon was an associate of Caniwi Capital Partners and Spheria Asset Management at the time he bought the shares, the filing added.

Grenon has recently demanded a board overhaul of NZME alleging the company of "poor" operating performance and "cherry picking" data to mislead investors.

NZME's shares fell 3% in recent Friday trade.

data.title: Carrefour Sweetens Takeover Offer for Brazilian Unit
data.description:
Carrefour said Thursday it increased its offer to buy all the outstanding shares of its Brazilian subsidiary, Grupo Carrefour Brasil.

The supermarket giant raised the offer to 8.50 Brazilian reals for each share of the unit from 7.70 reals per share previously.

Carrefour Brazil shareholders also have the option to receive one Carrefour share for every 9.96 shares held, compared with the previous option of one Carrefour share for every 11 Carrefour Brazil shares.

They can also choose to receive 4.25 reals in cash plus one Carrefour share for every 19.92 Carrefour Brazil shares, compared with every 22 shares previously.

Carrefour said the latest proposal is its best and final offer, and completion is expected before the end of the second quarter, subject to approval at a general meeting.

data.title: RBA Has Chance to Catch Up and Respond to Global StormsAnalysis
data.description:
By James Glynn

SYDNEY--The Reserve Bank of Australia missed a golden opportunity to shine this week when it kept interest rates on hold instead of responding meaningfully to the fire storm that now envelops the global economy and international markets.

It should have cut interest rates to make policy settings less restrictive, providing a buffer to the economy and confidence, instead of fussing about fading inflation risks while steering the bank wide of potential political entanglements ahead of a federal election on May 3.

RBA Governor Michele Bullock speaks a lot about the central bank's role of cautiously watching and waiting. This was a time for the central bank to do something. It now looks flat footed.

It was surprising to hear from Bullock at her press conference on Tuesday that the RBA's interest-rate setting board didn't even examine a scenario for a cut at its two-day meeting despite the shadow over the world's growth outlook.

Risks associated with the imminent dismantling of the international rules-based order that exists to smooth out trade disputes between countries were thrown around at the policy meeting, but it appears nobody was asked to vote on a cut.

Recent reforms of the RBA's board system were meant to encourage dissent to contest the central bank's narrative. Apparently that didn't happen.

It's not as if the Trump Administration had concealed its plans for far reaching reciprocal tariffs. Most of what has transpired since "Liberation Day" was eminently predictable.

To be sure, Washington's new trade regime has introduced a level of fear and loathing into markets that approaches conditions at the start of the Covid-19 pandemic, and worse, the collapse of market confidence during the global financial crisis in 2008.

The market fallout has come thick and fast, prompting JPMorgan to raise its probability of coming recession in the U.S. and the world economy to 60% from 40% on Friday.

The scope of the tariff wall being erected by the Trump White House is huge, but at the same time its nearly impossible to find any economic commentators or academics ready to offer a logical explanation for what Washington has done.

The overwhelming consensus is that the U.S. has kicked a monumental "own goal".

As markets tumble, some are hoping that the proposed tariffs can be renegotiated down to something that does far less to destroy global supply chains and business confidence.

But the tariffs appear more than just bargaining chips that the U.S. hopes will deliver it more favorable trade deals. It's far more likely that they will be a permanent part of the economic landscape going forward.

For the RBA, the ongoing ructions are stoking the case for a 50 basis point cut in the official cash rate in May.

Economists are now musing about the prospect of an emergency cut, while also adding to the number of cuts they expect this year.

"More aggressive RBA easing now seems more likely than not. We would not rule out a 50 basis point cut in May if sentiment sours and the global growth outlook deteriorates sufficiently," said Adam Boyton, head of Australian economics at ANZ.

ANZ now expects the RBA to cut interest rates three times by August.

This is a big test for the RBA, and the spotlight will fall heavily on Bullock when she delivers a speech to the Chief Executive Women Melbourne Annual Dinner in Melbourne on Thursday.

So as events change, Bullock will need to update markets directly over how the bank assesses the international environment, and lay out the policy options before the central bank.

A question or two might also be asked about why the board didn't kick around the idea of a cut this week knowing that the international economy was facing huge new challenges.

The lags involved in monetary policy means any impact of a lower official cash rate in late May might not arrive to support business activity until later this year or early in 2026.

Bullock can deal the RBA back into the game.

Write to James Glynn at james.glynn@wsj.com

data.title: Response Plus Unit, Falcon Aviation Services to Establish Air Ambulance Services in UAE
data.description:
Response Plus Holding's (ADX:RPM) subsidiary Response Plus Medical Services and Falcon Aviation Services agreed to collaborate on the development of air ambulance capabilities in the United Arab Emirates.

Under a signed memorandum of understanding, both companies will work together to enable transport of patients abroad for critical care treatments and streamline their return to UAE, according to a Thursday release.

data.title: Estrella Enters Partnership to Advance Drilling at Timor-Leste Project; Shares Fall 12%
data.description:
Estrella Resources has partnered with Dili-based CoreSearch Minerals and Mining Services to advance its drilling operations in the company's mineral prospects in Timor-Leste, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

The partnership follows Estrella's $200,000 investment to help CoreSearch import drilling equipment, which will be repaid through drilling services at $10 per meter drilled over the first 20,000 meters, the filing said.

Under the deal, CoreSearch will conduct 10,000 meters of reverse circulation drilling and 3,000 meters of diamond drilling, with a further 10,000 meters of drilling scheduled for later, the filing added.

Environmental approvals are nearing completion, with drilling expected to begin in mid-April, the company said.

Shares of the company fell 12% in recent Friday trade.

data.title: Eni Unit Launches Business Program for Congolese Entrepreneurs
data.description:
Eni Joule, Eni's (ENI.MI) school of entrepreneurship, launched a business incubation and training program in Congo to support 35 entrepreneurs.

The initiative aims to cultivate Congolese entrepreneurship in energy transition and decarbonization to drive local economic growth, the Italian energy group said Thursday.

data.title: Torrent Power Forms New Step-Down Subsidiary
data.description:
Torrent Power has incorporated a step-down subsidiary, named Torrent Urja 38, for electricity generation from renewable energy, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

Torrent Urja 38 is a special-purpose vehicle wholly owned by Torrent Green Energy, a wholly owned subsidiary of the company.

The new company will deal in hydrogen, its by-products and or its derivatives like ammonia, chemicals, compressed natural gas, and mineral substances.

The company could also deal in the generation and distribution of other energy sources such as compressed natural gas and mineral substances, and other forms of non-conventional, renewable, green, and hydropower, among others including electrical power and energy in all aspects.

data.title: PWC China Plans To Spin Off Cyber Security Arm- FT
data.description:
• PWC CHINA PLANS TO SPIN OFF CYBER SECURITY ARM- FT

data.title: Iveco Group Forms EUR2 Billion Medium Term Note Program
data.description:
Italian vehicle maker Iveco Group (IVG.MI) developed an up to 2 billion-euro medium term note program, according to a Thursday release.

Notes issued under the Central Bank of Ireland-approved program will be listed and traded on the regulated market of Euronext Dublin.
